[Speaker Notes] Enzyme names

Quick note on gluconeogenesis
Think: “fasting state” (6-8 after a meal)
Making of glucose from pyruvate
Making of glucose from “ glucogenic ” substrates
Some amino acids, oxaloacetate, lactate
NOT acetyl-CoA
Requires energy (typically from FA breakdown)
Takes place in the liver not in the brain or muscles
Glucose made in the liver: released into the blood to maintain blood glucose level

Lactate or Acetyl CoA
Lactate-
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)
NADH→ NAD + to keep glycolysis working
Lactate produced by RBCs and active muscles goes to liver for gluconeogenesis

Acetyl coenzyme A by pyruvate dehydrogenase in the mitochondria and utilized through TCA cycle

Cori Cycle: why the liver is your best friend
Liver can carryout gluconeogenesis, muscles cannot
low oxygen
conditions

Acetyl coenzyme A uses
TCA cycle
Acetyl-CoA + Oxaloacetate → Citrate
Fatty acid synthesis
Ketone body synthesis
Remember that Acetyl CoA is NOT a substrate of gluconeogenesis
Conversion of pyruvate to Acetyl-CoA is a committed step towards:
energy production (TCA cycle) or
storage (Acetyl-CoA is used for FA production)
[Speaker Notes] Carbohydrate synthesis from Acetyl CoA???

The fate of pyruvate
Pyruvate → ox aloacetate by pyruvate carb ox ylase
In the liver NOT in the muscles
Important reaction in:
Provide substrates for gluconeogenesis
Replenish intermediate of the TCA cycle
Pyruvate to alanine
Alanine cycle during fasting, major substrate for gluconeogenesis

The Alanine cycle:
Another reason why the lover is your best friend

Pyruvate Dehydrogenase

Pyruvate Dehydrogenase (PDH)
Multi-subunit enzyme complex
CoA-SH + NAD + Pyruvate  Acetyl-CoA + NADH + CO 2
Regulation:
Allosterically activated by Fructose1,6-bisPhos.
Inhibited by Acetyl-CoA and NADH
PDH kinase (converts PDH to phospho -PDH):
Phospho -PDH = less active
Activated by NADH, acetyl –CoA
Inhibited by NAD, CoA-SH
PDH phosphatase:
Activated by Ca ++ (muscle contraction)

Inhibited by:
ATP, NADH, Succinyl CoA

The Tricarboxylic Acid Cycle
α- ketoglutarate dehydrogenase plays an important role in maintaining the levels of the neurotransmitters glutamate, gamma–aminobutyric acid (GABA), and aspartate, as well as in protein synthesis
Know that α- ketoglutarate dehydrogenase requires thiamine (vitamin B1) as a cofactor

α- Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase
Inhibition of α-KGDH results in accumulation of α-KG
α-KG is a substrate for conversion to:
Glutamate
Aspartate
γ -amino butyric acid (GABA)
Result:
imbalance in neurotransmitter levels
Neurological disorder resulting in confabulation
Long term alcohol use: thiamine (B1) deficiency
α-KGDH requires thiamine for function
Neurotransmitters

thiamine (vitamin B1)
Know that thiamine is a cofactor for:
α- ketoglutarate dehydrogenase
Pyruvate dehydrogenase
Transketolase (in pentose phosphate pathway)
The result of these reactions is the production of NADH which will generate ATP (deficiency → reduced capacity of cells to generate energy)
